game_name: King's Quest: Quest for the Crown

design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore fantasy kingdom, locate and combine inventory items to solve environmental puzzles, defeat obstacles through lateral thinking to retrieve three treasures and earn kingship
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (limited inventory, one-way puzzle solutions) and permadeath risk creating high stakes for puzzle-solving decisions
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a rightful king by proving resourcefulness and cunning rather than combat prowess; adventure through magical realms as a common knight ascending to royalty
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Refuses to hold player's hand—expects environmental observation and trial-and-error experimentation; punishes inefficient inventory management and wrong puzzle sequences with game-over states; emphasizes sensory interaction (look, listen, smell, taste, touch) as core mechanical principle rather than tutorial guidance

difficulty.lives_system: Single life with permadeath on fatal mistakes (e.g., wrong item use, moving into hazards)
difficulty.checkpointing: No checkpointing; death requires full game restart from beginning
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal—difficulty is baked into design philosophy; players must navigate puzzle logic and resource management without difficulty settings or assistance

progression.upgrade_system: Inventory-based progression—accumulating correct items gates access to new areas and puzzle solutions; no stat progression
progression.unlock_mechanism: Three mandatory treasures (MacGuffins) unlock kingship; finding treasures requires solving region-specific puzzles in open exploration areas
replayability_hooks: Multiple solution paths for certain puzzles reward experimentation
Maze navigation and labyrinth mapping vary by playthrough
Item discovery order affects challenge perception

world_building_techniques: Environmental design establishes fantasy kingdoms without exposition
Magic mirror serves as narrative device for quest framing
Character encounters (witches, creatures) imply larger world mythology
Player discovery of NPCs and locations creates emergent narrative understanding

boss_design.philosophy: Environmental hazards and magical antagonists (witches) function as puzzle encounters rather than combat confrontations; victory through clever item usage or avoidance rather than skill execution
notable_bosses: Witch Dahlia—primary antagonist requiring specific item-based solution
Environmental hazards (dragons, creatures)—static obstacles demanding lateral thinking

faction_indicators: Kingdom of Daventry as political entity
Royal lineage (King Edward, succession through quest)
Magical realm inhabitants (witches, wizards) as external threat class
danger_signals: Aging/dying monarch (King Edward) creating succession vacuum
Tyrannical magic-users (Dahlia, witches) as destabilizing forces
Outsider protagonist (common knight) proving legitimacy through trial
environmental_storytelling: Castle ruins and abandoned structures suggest fallen kingdoms
Magical barriers and cursed lands indicate supernatural conflict history
NPC placement (guards, enchanters) reveals power distribution
Treasure locations hint at previous heroes' failures or achievements
